coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of modified PANI/CQDs nanocomposite by dimethylgyloxime for removal of Ni (II) from aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: El-Sawy, Abdelhamid M.
Abdo, Moa’mena H.
Darweesh, M.A.
Salahuddin, Nehal A.
coredata.dc:description: Polyaniline/carbon quantum dots (PANI/CQDs) nanocomposites were synthesized by simple interfacial polymerizations of aniline followed by adding different contents of carbon quantum dots (1,10 wt. %) to the polyaniline. The resulting (PANI/CQDs) nanocomposites were impregnated by dimethylgyloxime (DMG) and used as adsorbent to remove nickel ions (Ni II) from aquous solution. The nanocomposites were characterized using different techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR). A highly ordered crystalline nanosheets structure of PANI decorated by CQDs completely coated with DMG is obtained. The developed PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposite using 1,10 wt. % of CQDs have a high adsorption capacity of 290.3 and 245 mg/g, respectively. Several effects were studied to optimize the removal efficiency. Thermodynamic parameters such as entropy changes, enthalpy changes, and Gibbs free energy were evaluated. The adsorption kinetics of Ni (II) ions onto PANI/10-CQDs/DMG follows the second-order kinetic model. The adsorption isotherm models followed Langmuir isotherm. The developed promised nanocomposite is cheap, has high adsorption capacity, and reusable.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Wastewater treatment
Nanocomposite
Polyaniline/carbon quantum dots
Dimethylgyloxime
Adsorption
Removal of Ni(II)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of interfacial polymerization of polyaniline (PANI)
Removal of nickel ions using PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposite
Effect of temperature on the adsorption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FTIR spectra of PANI, CQDs, PANI/10-CQDs, DMG and PANI/10-CQDs/DMG (A1), PANI/10-CQDs/DMG after loading and release Ni (II) (A2).
XRD patterns of PANI, CQDs, PANI /10- CQDs and PANI /10- CQDs / DMG nanocomposites.
SEM micrograph of PANI (A1, A2), PANI/10-CQDs (B1, B2) and PANI/10-CQDs/DMG (C1, C2) at different magnifications.
The Removal (%) of Ni (II) vs time using PANI, PANI/DMG, PANI/(1,10)-CQDs, PANI/(1,10)-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite.
The effect of nickel ion concentrations on removal efficiency and adsorption capacityusing PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite, (A1) and PANI/1-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite. (A2).
Effect of different amounts of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite (A1), PANI/1-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite (A2) on the removal efficiency and the adsorption capacity of nickel ions.
Effect of temperature on the efficiency of removal and adsorption capacity qe (mg/g) of nickel ion onto PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite (A1) and PANI/1-CQDs/DMG composite (A2).
Van't Hoff plot for Ni (II) adsorption onto PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite.
Effect of pH on the release of Ni (II) ions from PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite.
Freundlich isotherm (A) Langmuir isotherm (B) Temkin (C) Dubinin-Radushkevich isotherm (D) for adsorption nickel ions onto PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite.

(A) Adsorption kinetic of pseudo-first order model. (B) Adsorption kinetic of pseudo-second order model. (C) The intraparticle diffusion model for adsorption process of Ni(II) ions onto PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite.
Isotherm parameters for Nickel ion adsorption onto PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite.
Kinetics parameters of different adsorption models of nickel ions onto the surface of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite.
Removal (%) and adsorption capacity of nickel ions with different adsorbents.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of modified PANI/CQDs nanocomposite by dimethylgyloxime for removal of Ni (II) from aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Tanta University, Tanta, 31527 Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aChemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Tanta University, Tanta, 31527 Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Basic Sciences Department, the Higher Institute of Engineering, Kafr El-Sheikh, 31527 Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bBasic Sciences Department, the Higher Institute of Engineering, Kafr El-Sheikh, 31527 Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics and Mathematical Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, Tanta University, Tanta, 31527 Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cPhysics and Mathematical Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, Tanta University, Tanta, 31527 Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyaniline/carbon quantum dots (PANI/CQDs) nanocomposites were synthesized by simple interfacial polymerizations of aniline followed by adding different contents of carbon quantum dots (1,10 wt. %) to the polyaniline. The resulting (PANI/CQDs) nanocomposites were impregnated by dimethylgyloxime (DMG) and used as adsorbent to remove nickel ions (Ni II) from aquous solution. The nanocomposites were characterized using different techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR). A highly ordered crystalline nanosheets structure of PANI decorated by CQDs completely coated with DMG is obtained. The developed PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposite using 1,10 wt. % of CQDs have a high adsorption capacity of 290.3 and 245 mg/g, respectively. Several effects were studied to optimize the removal efficiency. Thermodynamic parameters such as entropy changes, enthalpy changes, and Gibbs free energy were evaluated. The adsorption kinetics of Ni (II) ions onto PANI/10-CQDs/DMG follows the second-order kinetic model. The adsorption isotherm models followed Langmuir isotherm. The developed promised nanocomposite is cheap, has high adsorption capacity, and reusable.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution with heavy metals remains a significant environmental issue due to its negative impact on health and ecosystems [1]. Nickel ions exist in the environment by small concentration; however, a high concentration of nickel ions has a negative effect on the human due to its toxicity. Nickel ion has many industrial and technological applications such as batteries, electrocatalytic oxidation reactions, corrosion resistance, and making alloys such as stainless steel. Nickel metal has a lot of industrial applications, such as a catalyst for the hydrogenation of vegetable oils and glass processing, where the addition of nickel to glass gives a green color. Increasing the industrial demands and growing human population, the exploitation of natural resources leads to increased nickel ion as waste byproducts [2]. Nickel ions cause fatal diseases such as lung cancer, dermatitis, and allergic. Industrial wastewater contains Ni (II) ions in high concentration with a range of 2–200 mg·L−1 however, the legal limits of nickel ions concentration for drinking water are 0.02 mg·L−1 [3].

Various methods have been used for removing Ni(II), such as reverse osmosis, floatation, coagulation, electrodialysis, adsorption, and ion exchange. The adsorption process is a versatile and energy-efficient method for removing divalent nickel from wastewater. Several materials have been explored to remove nickel ions, e.g. mesoporous materials, metal/metal oxides, activated carbon, and polymers [4].

Polyaniline (PANI) has wide applications due to its easy synthesis, low production cost, eco-friendly, has thermal stability, and non-toxic nature [5]. The nitrogen groups in PANI can adsorb the metal cations in their neutral state. The doping forms have a positive charge nature leading to repel the cations [6]. Pristine polyaniline was used as an adsorbent to eliminate different toxic metals such as lead ions with high removal efficiency (47% using 50 mg·L−1) [7]. Polyaniline/CoFeC6N6 nanocomposite was used for removing of the nickel ions (37% using 100 mg·L−1) [8]. The polyaniline modified with antimony oxide adsorbed Hg (II) in 50 min (99% using 50 mg·L−1) [9].

CQDs are small tiny carbon nanoparticles with particle sizes less than 10 nm [10]. It has many features such as optical properties, electronic [11] and physicochemical characteristics, low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and easy synthetic routes [10]. CQDs can be easily produced in large-scale, and it can easy functionalized. The CQDs has a high surface area, and it is biocompatible [11]. CQDs have many wide applications such as nanomedicine, chemical sensing, bioimaging, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, adsorption of heavy metals or dyes [10] and are used as fluorescent probes to detect mercury [12]. Recently, CQDs/ZnAl-LDH was used to adsorb Cd (II) [11].

The commercially adsorbent materials such as citric acid and starch were used to remove the nickel ions. These materials suffer from low adsorption efficiency and cannot be reused several times [13]. Dimethylgyloxime can selectively interact with nickel ions to form a complex [14]. The DMG has functional groups of -C(NOH)- and oxime that can react with the Ni (II) ions. The oxime is the main cause of colorimetric detection for the trace amount of Ni (II) ions [15]. DMG was applied for coloring Ni (II) ions, a red colored chelate is formed by a chelating reaction between Ni (II) and DMG, and was removed by surfactant-modified ZSM-5 nano- zeolite with high removal efficiency (80% at time 60 min for 5 mg·L−1 concentration) [16]. The DMG-modified zeolite Y from diatomite was used to remove Ni (II) (99.87 % in 9 h from 100 mg·L−1 concentration) [17]. Dimethylglyoxime -modified Amberlite IRA-420 was used to remove nickel ions from an aqueous solution (90% in 10 min for the concentration of 15.75 mg·L−1). The adsorption capacity of nickel ion was improved when Polyethylene imine was modified by DMG [18]. The petroleum samples treated by a mesoporous composite of silica-DMG showed high removal efficiency for nickel ions [15]. The waste of coal fly-ash can be used as a source for calcium silicate to remove the Ni, Cu and Zn ions by percentage 96.98%, 60.75%, and 30.47%, respectively, using an initial ion concentration of 400 mg·L−1after surface treatment by DMG [19]. The graphene nanosheets and zinc oxide nanoparticles treated by DMG revealed an adsorption capacity of 80% from 20 mg·L−1of nickel ion in 1 h [20]. The flower globular magnesium hydroxide was used to remove nickel ions with a removal efficiency of 92.64% within 50 min using an initial concentration of 80 mg·L−1 [21]. Herein, the PANI/CQDs nanocomposites modified by DMG was used to remove the Ni(II) [22]. PANI was prepared by interfacial polymerization of aniline (heterogeneous biphasic system) using the previous procedure [23] with some modification, including the organic solvent used (toluene) followed by adding different contents of CQDs into the polymer and direct immobilization of DMG into the polymer and nanocomposites. The developed nanocomposite of PANI/CQDs/DMG was used to remove nickel ions from an aqueous solution. The FTIR, XRD, SEM, and the efficiency of the adsorption were studied. Some parameters such as time contact, different concentrations of Ni (II), dose, and temperature for removing nickel ions from aqueous solution were studied. Adsorption thermodynamics, Adsorption isotherm, and kinetic adsorption were investigated.The efficiency of polyaniline is improved by CQDs for adsorption of nickel ions. The developed nanocomposite is cheap, high adsorption capacity, and reusable.

Aniline monomer (98%) was double distilled before its use. Citric acid, toluene, and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Inna chemicals. Ammonium persulfate was purchased from Fisher chemicals. Dimethylgyloxime (DMG) and Eriochrome black T were purchased from Aldrich chemicals. Ethanol, hydrochloric acid, and nickel sulfate were purchased from Nasr chemicals Company. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of interfacial polymerization of polyaniline (PANI)
ce:section.ce:para: PANI was prepared using interfacial polymerization technique following the reported method with slight modification [23]. Accordingly, the organic phase contains 2.976 g aniline was sonicated in 100 mL toluene for 60 min. The aqueous phase includes 1.825 g ammonium persulfate in 100 mL of HCl (1 M). Then, the aqueous solution was poured onto the organic phase, and the immiscible biphasic system is formed between aniline and aqueous medium. The reaction of PANI was stirred at 30 °C for 24 h, the product was washed by water/ethanol and dried for 24 h at 50 °C in the oven (yield: 1.99 g).

PANI/DMG nanocomposite was synthesized by the addition of DMG to PANI. Accordingly, DMG (0.03 g) was dissolved in 30 mL ethanol and added to 0.5 g of PANI with vigorous stirring for 24 h and dried at 30 °C to afford PANI/DMG (yield = 0.51 g).

The CQDs were prepared according to Benjamin C. M. Martindale's method [24]. The citric acid (100 g) was melted at 180 °C for 40 h under atmospheric air. The color of melted citric acid was transformed from a colorless liquid to yellow, orange, and brown. A highly viscous liquid was obtained after cooling the liquid. The obtained liquid was allowed to stir in distilled water (100 mL). NaOH (5 M, 50 mL) was used to adjust the pH to be neutral to obtain an orange-brown solution. The obtained CQDs was capped with sodium carboxylate [24]. The freeze-drying was used to isolate a yellow-orange product for 48 h (yield = 40 g).

PANI/1-CQDs nanocomposite was synthesized by adding CQDs (0.01 g) dispersed in 30 mL distilled water into solution (30 mL) of PANI (1 g) and sonicated. The product was dried for 24 h at 50 °C in the oven (yield: 1.00 g). Then, DMG (0.03 g) dissolved in 30 mL ethanol was added to 0.5 g of PANI/1-CQDs and stirred vigorously for 24 h, filtered and dried at 30 °C (yield = 0.52 g). The same procedure was followed to prepare PANI/10-CQDs using 0.1 g of CQDs followed by impregnating the product into DMG (0.03 g) dissolved in 30 mL ethanol to afford PANI/10-CQDs/DMG.

The nickel ions are prepared from nickel sulfate (NiSO4.8H2O) at different concentrations (300 mg·L−1, 500 mg·L−1, 700 mg·L−1 and 900 mg·L−1). The solution was colored with 0.1 mL of Eriochrome black T (0.02 M) using excess buffer base solution to convert the solution to basic medium and the removal efficiency was calculated during 5–100 min by using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer at λmax = 393 nm.

The X-ray diffraction was measured using GNR APD-2000 PRO, coupled by monochromatized Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å), a generator voltage 40 kV and current 30 mA at a scan rate of 8 deg/min and a scan range is 5-80o. The morphology was examined by scanning electron microscope (JEOL-JSM-6510 TV). The FTIR spectra were measured by a Shimadzu FTIR-8400S in the range of 4000-400 cm−1 with an accuracy of 2 cm−1 and 64 scans the KBr disc was used during the measurement with 13 mm-diameter pellets). The concentration of colored nickel ions was monitored using the UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Labomed, UVD-2960).

The adsorbent samples (0.03 g) were magnetically stirred with 20 ml of a certain amount of Ni(II) solution. The nickel ion concentration was evaluated using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer at 393 nm. The sorption capacity (qt) was evaluated by Eq. (1)
(1)
q t
=
(
C o
−
C t
)
V / m

“Co” is the nickel ions initial concentration “mg·L−1”, “Ct” is the concentration of nickel ion at time “t” “mg·L−1”, “V” is the volume of nickel ion “L”, and “m” is the mass in gram of adsorbent [25].

The percentage of Ni (II) removal (%) was calculated using the following Eq. (2).
(2)
Removal
( % )
=
[
(
C o
−
C e
)
/
C o
]
x 100

Many factors that affect the adsorption capacity and removal efficiency were examined, such as nickel ion concentration “mg·L−1”, absolute temperature “K”, contact time “min”, and adsorbent mass [26].

The adsorption kinetics was studied using 0.03 g of PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposite in 20 ml nickel ion solution (500 mg·L−1) at pH = 7. The adsorption was always carried out via incubator thermostat shaker. The reaction occurred in a water bath with a temperature controller where the temperature is controlled 25 ± 2 °C, 200 rpm. The solution was colored with 0.1 ml of Eriochrome black T (0.02M) using excess buffer base solution to convert the solution to a basic medium. The removal efficiency was calculated during 5-100 min by using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer at λmax = 393 nm [27,28]. The isotherm was studied using the temperature range (303-318 K) at the same concentrations.

FTIR spectra of polyaniline (PANI), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), PANI/10-CQDs, dimethylgyloxime (DMG), PANI/10-CQDs/DMG, PANI/10-CQDs/DMG after loading Ni (II), and PANI/10-CQDs/DMG after release Ni (II) (Fig. 1 ). FTIR spectrum of the pristine PANI showed the main characteristic peaks for PANI, such as N-H stretching vibration band at 3438 cm−1 , stretching vibrations band of CH aromatic ring attaching by NH stretching at 2363 cm−1 and stretching vibrations band of benzenoid moieties in PANI (CC) at 1578 cm−1, stretching vibrations band of quinoid moieties in PANI (CN) at 1491 cm−1, -CN stretching vibrations band in the secondary aromatic amines at 1306 cm−1, CH stretching vibrations band at 1127 cm−1 and CH bending band of benzenoid unit at 817 cm−1 [29]. FTIR spectrum of the pristine CQDs showed the OH stretching band's located at 3705 cm−1, OH bonded at 3340 cm−1, CH2 symmetric band at 2931 cm−1, CH2 asymmetric band at 2770 cm−1, the CH stretching vibration at 2700 cm−1 [30], OH stretching (carboxylic acid) at 2589 cm−1, CO stretching band at 1526 cm−1, CO or CC stretching band at 1386 cm−1[30,31] and CH bending band at 903 cm−1 [32,33]. For PANI/10-CQDs the band identified for NH stretching vibration in PANI at 3438 cm−1 was shifted to lower wavenumber 3436 cm−1, OH bonded in CQDs at 3340 cm−1 was shifted to lower wavenumber 3233 cm−1, the CH stretching vibration in CQDs at 2700 cm−1 [30] was shifted to higher wavenumber 2803 cm−1, OH stretching (carboxylic acid) in CQDs at 2589 cm−1 was shifted to lower wavenumber 2578 cm−1, stretching vibrations band of CH aromatic ring attaching by NH stretching in PANI at 2363 cm−1 was shifted to higher wavenumber 2373 cm−1, the stretching vibrations band of benzenoid moieties in PANI of CC was shifted to lower wavenumber 1568 cm−1, CN stretching vibrations band of quinoid moieties in PANI was shifted to lower wavenumber 1482 cm−1, CN stretching vibrations band in PANI at 1306 cm−1 was shifted to lower wavenumber 1300 cm−1, CH stretching vibrations band in PANI at 1127 cm−1 was shifted to lower wavenumber 1117 cm−1 and CH bending band was shifted to lower wavenumber 816 cm−1. More detailed assignment of different peaks of the FTIR spectra of PANI, CQDs, PANI/10-CQDs (Table S1). FTIR spectrum of DMG reveals the band at 3215 cm−1 corresponding to the OH stretching vibrations, CC stretching vibrations band at 1442 cm−1, CNO bending band at 707 cm−1, and CH banding band at 900 cm−1. FTIR spectrum PANI/10-CQDs/DMG revealed that the band characteristic to NH stretching of PANI/10-CQDs at 3436 cm−1 was shifted to 3457 cm−1, the stretching band of OH of PANI/10-CQDs was shifted to 3126, the stretching band of CC of PANI/10-CQDs was shifted to 1569 cm−1, CN stretching band of PANI/10-CQDs was shifted to 1492 cm−1 and bending band of CH of PANI/10-CQDs was shifted to 741 cm−1confirming the interaction between PANI, CQDs, and DMG. FTIR spectrum ofPANI/10-CQDs/DMG after loading Ni(II) revealed that the sharp band characteristic to NH stretching of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG at 3457 cm−1 was shifted to broad peak at 3293 cm−1 and the stretching vibrations band of benzenoid moieties in PANI/10-CQDs/DMG (CC) at 1569 cm−1 was shifted to high wavenumber 1627 cm−1, this is confirming the loading of nickel ions on the nanocomposite. FTIR spectrum of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG after release Ni (II) revealed that the band characteristic to NH stretching of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG at 3457 cm−1 was shifted to 3435 cm−1 and the stretching vibrations band of benzenoid moieties in PANI/10-CQDs/DMG (CC) at 1569 cm−1 was shifted to high wavenumber 1576 cm−1, this is meaning that almost the nanocomposite returned as same before the Ni (II) loading [34]

XRD patterns of PANI, CQDs, PANI/10-CQDs and PANI/10-CQDs/DMG are shown in Fig. 2 . XRD pattern of PANI showed three sharp peaks at 2θ = 18.02°, 19.88°, 23.68° which is marked by dark stars in Fig. 2. The characteristic diffraction peaks are assigned to (011), (020), and (200) crystal planes of PANI, respectively, with an orthorhombic structure. The same peaks were observed previously with a slight shift upon using chloroform for preparation PANI by interfacial polymerization. The crystallite size was calculated to be 19.7 nm [23]. It was reported that the characteristic polyaniline emeraldine salt peaks were observed at 20.1o or 25.1o [35]. The presence of many crystalline peaks and the characteristic peaks were attributed to the crystallization of organic molecules in the polymer matrix [35]. The diffraction pattern of CQDs revealed three peaks at 2θ = 12°, 15°and 25° which is marked by red squares in the Fig. 2. The XRD pattern of PANI/10-CQDs nanocomposite shows some peaks of PANI and CQDs as indexed in Fig. 2, confirming the formation of PANI/10-CQDs. For PANI/10-CQDs/DMG, no peaks were observed due to a complete coating of polyaniline/CQDs nanoparticles by the DMG but a small broad peak of CQDs was appeared at 2θ = 25°.

The surface morphology of PANI, PANI/10-CQDs, and PANI/10-CQDs/ DMG was examined by SEM, Fig. 3 . The SEM images of PANI (A1, A2) showed aggregated nanosheets of less than 200 nm. In PANI/10-CQDs it is clearly to observe CQDs deposited on the surface of PANI with an average diameter of 30 nm. SEM image of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG (Fig. 3(C1, C2)) reveals DMG coverage onto the surface of PANI/10-CQDs, which is consistent with the XRD pattern.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of nickel ions using PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Different parameters were studied, such as the effect of nickel ion concentration, contact time, the dose of adsorbent, and temperature were tested to remove the Ni(II) from the aqueous solution to obtain a maximum sorption efficiency [21,36–39].

The removal efficiency of PANI for Ni (II) was recorded to be 21.98% within 5 min and reached the equilibrium (65.94%) within 55 min. PANI/DMG improved the removal efficiency to be 38.8% within 5 min, and the equilibrium was reached (49.43 %) within 15 min due to the selectivity properties for nickel ion by DMG [15]. The removal efficiency of nickel ions by PANI/1-CQDs nanocomposite was detected to be (38.54%) within 5 min and reached the equilibrium at (69.9%) in 40 min. Increasing the percentage of CQDs in PANI/10-CQDs, improved the uptake to (51.94%) within 5 min, and the equilibrium was reached (81.9%) within 40 min as shown in Fig. 4 . The capacity of adsorption was improved by impregnation with DMG due to the presence of anionic form in oxime [40]. The uptake of nickel ions using PANI/1-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite was estimated to be 60% within 5 min and reached the equilibrium (72.5%) in 15 min. The uptake of nickel ions was improved by using PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite to be 73.3% within 5 min and reached the equilibrium (86%) within 15 min. It was reported that the nano-kaolinite showed favorable adsorption capacity for Ni (II) ions in comparison with kaolinite and activated carbon [41].

The adsorption of different nickel ion concentrations (300 mg·L−1, 500 mg·L−1, 700 mg·L−1, and 900 mg·L−1) on PANI/1-CQDs/DMG and PANI/10-CQDs/DMG surface was studied (Fig. 5 ). The removal efficiency of PANI/1-CQDs/DMG using different nickel ions concentrations 300 mg·L−1, 500 mg·L−1, 700 mg·L−1, and 900 mg·L−1 was assessed to be 75.36 %, 72.7 %, 65.9 %, and 57.3%, and the capacity of adsorption was estimated to be 152, 245, 308.2 and 344.3 mg/g, respectively. By increasing the CQD content in PANI/10-CQDs/DMG, the removal efficiency was improved to be 93.3%, 86.3%, 70.3%, and 67.2 %, and the capacity of adsorption enhanced to 188.3, 290.3, 328.5, and 403.5 mg/g, respectively. The removal efficiency increases when the amount of nickel ion decreases per unit mass of adsorbent. Decreasing the efficiency may be explained by decreasing in the available active sites of the nanocomposite that is available for the adsorption.

The influence of different amounts of PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposite (0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07 and 0.09 g) on removal efficiency was studied at 300 K (500 mg·L−1) as shown in Fig. 6 . By increasing the amount of adsorbent, the removal efficiency was improved. With increasing the amount of PANI/1-CQDs/DMG, the removal efficiency increased to be 61.53%, 72.7%, 79.3 %, 85.06% and 90.93%, respectively, and for PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite, the removal efficiency was determined to be 83.09%, 86.3%, 90.17%, 92.97%, and 95.41 %, respectively. The adsorption capacity of PANI/1-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite was enhanced to be 207, 245, 267, 286, and 306 mg/g with increasing the amounts of nanocomposite from 0.01 to 0.09 g. The adsorption capacity for PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite was estimated to be 279.5, 290.3, 303.3, 313 and 321 mg/g by using 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07 and 0.09 g respectively as shown in Fig. 6 (A1, A2) [42]. The increasing efficiency of adsorption was attributed to the availability of the adsorption sites of the nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on the adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature was studied on the removal efficiency of PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposite at 298, 303, 308, 313, and 318 Kas shown in Fig. 7 . The removal efficiency of Ni (II) ion by using PANI/1-CQDs/DMG was increased from 72.72% to 91.3 %, and the adsorption capacity was increased from 245 to 307 mg/g when the temperature increased from 298 to 318 K. For PANI/10-CQDs/DMG the removal (%) increased from 86.3% to 97 % and the adsorption capacity was increased from 290.3 to 327 mg/g by increasing the temperature confirming that the physical adsorption is an endothermic process [43].

The plot of Van't Hoff is shown in Fig. 8 . The parameters of thermodynamic such as ΔHo and ΔSo was calculated from the slope and intercept of Van't Hoff plot Eq. 3:
(3)
ln
(
q e
/
C e
)
=
Δ
S
o
/ R −
Δ
H
o
/ RT

The calculated value of thermodynamic parameters ΔHo and ΔSo are 63.92 kJmol−1 and 224.89 J mol−1 K−1, respectively. The value of ΔHo shows that the nickel ion adsorption process on the surface of the developed adsorbent is endothermic. [44–46] The positive value of ΔSo indicates the increase in the degree of randomness at the interface between the adsorbate solid surface and nickel solution [47].

At a different temperature of 298, 303, 308, 313, and 318 K, the Gibbs free energy [45,48] was evaluated using Eq. (4) to be -3.47, -4.16, -4.97, -6.12, and -8.14 kJ·mol−1, respectively.
(4)
Δ
G o
= − R T
l n

The Gibbs free energy has a negative value indicates that adsorption of nickel ions is a spontaneous process. The value of free energy decreased by increasing the temperature indicates that the adsorption of nickel ions on the surface of the nanocomposite is more favorable [49].

The release of nickel ion (500 mg·L−1) adsorbed on the surface of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG was studied. The release was improved from 91.6 % to 99.93 % within 60 min by changing the pH from 3 to 1, respectively (Fig. 9 ). The release of Ni (II) ions was increased with decreasing the pH. At low pH, the negative charge was neutralized, and the electrostatic attractions of Ni (II) ions were decreased [9].

Adsorption isotherms are described by different models, e.g., Freundlich, Langmuir, Dubinin-Radosevich (D-R), and Temkin isotherms. Adsorption of nickel ions on the PANI/10-CQDs/DMG surface can be explained by the relationship between adsorption capacity (qe) and equilibrium adsorbate concentration (Ce) in the liquid phase [45,50].

Freundlich isotherm describes the adsorption characteristics of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite as multilayer adsorption using the following empirical Eq. (5):
(5)
l n
q e
= l n
K f
+
(
1 / n
)
l n
C e

“Ce” is the equilibrium concentration of nickel ion (mg/L), “Kf” is Freundlich isotherm constant (mg/g), and “qe” is the amount of adsorbed nickel ion at equilibrium per gram of the adsorbent (mg/g) and n is adsorption intensity. The “n” value refers to the adsorption process favorability. The n value lies between one to ten, and the smaller “1/n” refer to heterogeneity surface of PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposite [8,12,13].

The Langmuir isotherm describes the formation of monolayer nickel ions on the outer surface of the PANI/10-CQD/DMG. The model suggested the formation of uniform energies of adsorption onto the surface of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG (Fig. 10 B) and described in the Eq. (6) [26].
(6)
1 /
q e
=
( 1 /
C e
K L
q max
)
+
( 1 /
q max
)

“qmax” is maximum monolayer coverage capacity (mg/g), “Ce” is the equilibrium concentration of (nickel ion) adsorbate (mg/L−1), “qe” is the amount of nickel ion adsorbed per gram of the adsorbent at equilibrium (mg/g), and “KL” is Langmuir isotherm constant (L/mg).

Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R) isotherm was used to study the adsorption mechanism of heterogeneous surfaces with Gaussian energy distribution. The Dubinin–Radushkevich model is based on the assumption that the mechanism of adsorption is more pore-filling instead of layer-by-layer surface coverage, which applies to the adsorption process involving only Van der Waals [51,52]. Fig. 10D depicts (D-R) isotherm model to describe adsorption on PANI/10-CQDs/DMG surface Eq. (7) [50]:
(7)
ln
(
q e
)
= ln
(
q s
)
−
(
K ad
ε
2
)

“Ɛ” is Dubinin–Radushkevich isotherm constant, “qs” is theoretical isotherm saturation capacity (mg/g); “qe” is the amount of adsorbate in the adsorbent at equilibrium(mg/g); and “Kad” is Dubinin–Radushkevich isotherm constant (mol2/kJ2) [53]. The “Ɛ” can be given by Eq. (8):
(8)
ε
= R T
l n
[
1 +
1 Ce
]
“Ce” is adsorbate equilibrium concentration (mg·L−1), “T” is the absolute temperature (K), and “R” is the gas constant (8.314 J/mol K), Fig. 10D.

Temkin model suggested a linear decrease in the adsorption energy with increasing the surface coverage of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite, as shown in Fig. 10C [51,54]. The model assumes that the function of the temperature of adsorbed molecules in the layer will not decrease logarithmic but decrease linearly with coverage; then, the model is defined in Eq. (9):
(9)
q e
=
B t
l n
K t
+
B t
l n
C e
(10)
q e
=
RT
B t
l n
A T
+
RT
B t
l n
C e

“R” is gas constant (8.314J/mol/K), “Bt” is Temkin isotherm constant which is related to the heat of sorption (J/mol), “AT” is Temkin isotherm equilibrium binding constant (L/g), and “T” is the absolute temperature at 298 K [47,53].

The results obtained by the different adsorption models Freundlich, Langmuir, Dubinin-Radnshkevich (D-R), and Temkin isotherm models were tabulated in Table 1 . The correlation coefficient (R2) value for Freundlich is lower (0.9255) than that for the Langmuir model (0.9980). This indicates that Langmuir isotherm is a suitable model for the adsorption of nickel ions on the surface of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG. Langmuir adsorption isotherm suggesting monolayer adsorption.
(11)
R L
= 1 / 1 + b
C O
b = Langmuir constant (L/mg), Co = initial concentration of Ni(II) ions (mg·L−1)

In the case of RL > 1 or =0, this means unfavorable and irreversible adsorption process occurred, respectively. If RL > 0, <1, this indicates a favorable adsorption process for Ni(II) removal. RL is calculated to be 0.03, revealing a favorable adsorption process. The Langmuir model revealed that the outer surface of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG was covered with a homogeneous monolayer of the nickel ion [44–46].
The dynamics of the adsorption process were investigated using the pseudo-first order, the pseudo-second order, and the intraparticle diffusion models [55,56].

The adsorption kinetics of the pseudo-first-order model was studied by Eq. (12):
(12)
l n
(
q e
−
q t
)
= l n
q e
−
k 1
t
qe is the amount of nickel ion adsorbed (mg/g) at equilibrium, and qt is the amount of nickel ion adsorbed (mg/g) at time t (min). K1 (min−1) is the equilibrium rate constant of the pseudo-first-order reaction. The correlation coefficient R2 of the pseudo-first-order model (Fig. 11 A) was found to be 0.9242, the calculated equilibrium adsorption capacities (qe values) were tabulated in Table 2 . The straight-line of the pseudo-first-order model was not valid in all contact time Fig. 11A [1,55].

The pseudo-second-order kinetic model was examined on the adsorption of Ni (II) onto PANI/10-CQDs/DMG Eq. (13) [55]:
(13)
(
t /
q t
)
=
(
1 /
k 2
q 2
e
)
+
(
t /
q e
)
k2 represents the pseudo-second-order model rate constant (g mol−1 min−1). The values of k2 and R2 values are provided in Table 2. The correlation coefficient R2 value of the pseudo-second-order model (0.9997) is better than the pseudo-first-order model Fig. 11B [1]. There are some references shows that the second-order kinetic model is a physical adsorption [44–46]. The calculated activation energy from Arrhenius equation can give the type of adsorption. The adsorption process is classified into three categories according to the value of activation energy. The first one is film-diffusion controlled when the activation energy is below 16 kJ/mol. The second one, when the value of Ea is in the range of 16–40 kJ/mol the adsorption process is particle-diffusion controlled. Finally, the chemical-reaction is controlled when E is greater than 40 kJ/mol. So, the adsorption process of Ni (II) onto PANI/10-CQDs/DMG is film-diffusion controlled due to the activation energy is below 16 kJ/mol equal to Ea = 5.74 kJ/mol confirming physical adsorption [57].

This model was applied to know the rate-limiting step of the adsorption process. During the adsorption, the nickel ion migrates from the bulk of solution to the surface of the PANI/10-CQDs/DMG, and then the nickel ion diffuses through the boundary layer to the surface of adsorbent. In addition, the adsorption rate depends on the speed at which nickel ions diffuses towards PANI/10-CQDs/DMG as shown in Fig. 11C. The intraparticle diffusion model can be explained using Eq. 14 [56]:
(14)
q t
=
k p
t
0.5
+ C

“C” is a constant that has information for the thickness of the boundary layer and the “kp” is rate constant of the intraparticle diffusion (mol/ g min0.5). The results demonstrated that the value of the correlation coefficient R2 for the pseudo-second order model is more fitted than the intraparticle diffusion and pseudo-first order models.

A comparative result of the maximum adsorption capacity “qmax” of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite with other reported adsorbents is shown in Table 3 . The largest value of “qmax” in PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposite was attributed to the main functional groups on the surface of adsorbent [41]. The adsorbent is promising versus other adsorbents reported for nickel ion adsorption.

The reusability was studied on the PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite by multiple adsorption-desorption cycles. The nickel ion was released from the PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite (0.03 gm) at pH = 1. The adsorption-desorption processes were repeated 9 times. The PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposite sustained its efficiency of removal (100% of nickel ions (500 mg·L−1) after 60 min) for 5 cycles, as shown in Fig. 12 . At cycle 6, the efficiency of removal decreased from 93.86% to 71.14% in cycle 9. This means that our developed nanocomposite can be used several times without losing its efficiency. On the contrary, the commercially available adsorbent of nickel ion can be used only for one time [13]. The commercial adsorbents and other known adsorbents for adsorbent of nickel ion have low removal efficiency compared to the PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite Table 3.

The removal of nickel ions by PANI/CQDs/DMG nanocomposites using different contents of CQDs was studied. Polyaniline was synthesized by interfacial polymerization followed by adding different contents of CQDs (1,10 wt.%) to afford the nanocomposites, and the surface was modified by DMG. The removal efficiency of Ni (II) depends on the dose of the nanocomposite, temperature, time contact, and nickel ion concentration. The adsorption process of nickel ions is a spontaneous and endothermic process in nature. The adsorption capacity (290.3 mg/g) of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite is higher than the other developed nanomaterials in the literature. The adsorption kinetics of nickel ions by PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite obeyed the pseudo-second order model and Langmuir isotherm model. This high efficiency of PANI/10-CQDs/DMG nanocomposite affords the nanocomposite out-front of the commercially available adsorbent for nickel ions. Moreover, this nanocomposite is cheap, and it can be easily large-scale up.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Abdelhamid M. El-Sawy: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Moa’mena H. Abdo: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. M.A. Darweesh: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Nehal A. Salahuddin: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2021.101392.
